title: Colona floribunda
intro: Colona floribunda, also known as the Koa haole or Island colona, is a flowering plant native to Hawaii, characterized by its fragrant white flowers and woody branches. The plant has been traditionally used in Hawaiian medicine for its health benefits, which include anti-inflammatory, antibacterial, and antifungal properties, making it effective in treating wounds, skin infections, and respiratory issues. The bioactive constituents of Colona floribunda include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In traditional Hawaiian medicine, the plant is often prepared as a tea or infused oil, and is also used in topical applications such as salves and ointments to harness its therapeutic benefits.
taxonomy: Colona floribunda is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Malvaceae. It is a member of the genus Colona, which is native to tropical regions of Asia and the Pacific. The genus Colona is part of the larger Malvaceae family, which includes over 200 genera of flowering plants. Colona floribunda is a shrub or small tree that produces clusters of small flowers, and is often found in woodland and coastal areas. The exact classification and ranking of Colona floribunda within the plant kingdom can be further refined through more specific botanical classification systems.

Its extracts and leaves have been used to treat various health issues, including fever, rheumatism, and skin problems.
